content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Or: the employee, the restaurant, and the casino.
Your employee just left on holiday
Running out of AI credits doesn’t feel like a service interruption, because AI isn’t a service anymore. Most of us are under some mandate — explicit or ambient — to use it extensively. It shapes not just how fast we work but how we think about the work. We’re not paying for a tool that supports our work; we’re paying for the work itself. We’ve become middlemen, employing AI to execute the day-to-day, on a stipend called credits.
Which is why hitting zero feels the way it does: your employee has announced a holiday, effective immediately, tasks unfinished, return date set by someone else’s calendar. Your only fallback is to do the work yourself, at the worst possible moment, because you’d planned around having help. And you never know when the next holiday is coming unless you check in every single day.
Once you see it that way, the interesting question stops being whether people use AI. It’s how well they manage a budget that already behaves like a currency: held as a balance, spent with every prompt, reset on a cycle whether or not you spent it well.
Not a utility bill. A stipend, with real consequences for spending it badly or running out early.
Spending a first pay check
Most users today spend like someone with their first pay check: bills covered, room left over, everything feeling slightly unbelievable. Outcomes arrive faster and better than expected, which makes the next prompt feel free. Spending stays uncritical until the balance says otherwise.
Behavioural Economics predicts most of what happens next. Nobody has the bandwidth to evaluate every prompt and model choice, so “good enough” becomes the default. A bad outcome that burns a chunk of the balance stings far more than a good one satisfies, so one failure breeds lasting over-caution. And people spend against today’s balance without asking whether it will last until the reset — until it doesn’t, once, and they swing to checking it after every message.
Two patterns come with actual evidence.
Price stands in for quality.
Users can’t directly evaluate how much better the premium model would be for their specific task, that information sits with the provider. So they do what buyers always do under information asymmetry: treat price as a proxy for quality, and match it to the stakes. Routine work goes cheap — documentation, cost guides, and gateway defaults all push it there. High-stakes work goes premium: developer traffic splits about 50/50 between flagship and mid-tier, and in Anthropic’s Economic Index, every additional $10 of hourly wage tied to a task adds 1.5–2.8 percentage points of premium-model use. People aren’t anchoring on a price tag. They’re budgeting by what the task is worth — which is investment behaviour, not consumption.
That’s not consumption behaviour. That’s investment behaviour.
Expiring credits are a gym membership.
In the canonical study, flat-fee gym members paid over $17 per visit when a $10-per-visit pass existed, forgoing about $600 each — people reliably overpay for access they don’t use. Monthly credit allotments that vanish at reset are the same contract. Which is why rollover, not a bigger allocation, is the fix that matters. Hold that thought.
The restaurant and the casino
Here’s where the pricing itself starts working against you, in two different ways.
First, the restaurant problem.
You order a coffee and get billed for a banquet. It happens because models have no memory: every turn, your entire conversation history is re-sent and re-charged as input. The first question in a chat costs a fraction of a cent. The same question, ten turns deep, costs ten times more because, the kitchen re-cooks everything you’ve eaten before serving anything new.
The kitchen re-cooks everything you’ve eaten before serving anything new.
Even developers who see itemised dollar costs can’t easily predict them; the meter is honest, but the thing it’s metering compounds invisibly. You ate little. The bill is huge. And nothing on the menu warned you.
Second, the casino problem.
Casinos convert cash to chips because people spend chips more freely. Abstraction dulls loss aversion, and a $5 chip never hurts like a $5 bill. Credit plans run on the same mechanic: the link between one credit and its dollar cost is deliberately fuzzy. API pricing is at least cash with a confusing bill. Credits are chips. And the further the abstraction stretches, the further spending drifts from what a fully informed user would choose.
Put the two together and you get the behaviour everyone recognises: assume the balance is fine until it isn’t, then overcorrect into checking it constantly. Some users chain a second model to optimise the other’s output, adding spend without fixing anything, because the tool cannot supply the context only the user holds.
Managing a budget blind
Strip the analogies away and three structural gaps remain.
Unpredictability
Neither the outcome nor its cost is knowable in advance.
No visibility
There’s no sense of what normal usage looks like, and no forecast of remaining allocation against upcoming needs.
Rigid ceilings
Hard caps don’t remove friction, they relocate it — into a justification email to finance and a day of waiting, or into the quiet sense that a raised limit is a penalty for using the system as intended.
Users are managing an investment budget with none of the tools an investor gets.
The tools exist. The trust doesn’t.
Most of the fix already ships. Enterprise gateways (LiteLLM, Portkey, OpenRouter) route requests to cheaper capable models and give teams real-time spend visibility. Personal tools (TypingMind, LibreChat) attach a running cost meter to every response and let you cap yourself. Semantic caching stops you re-paying for questions you’ve effectively already asked.
But the middleman layer has its own bill: an extra network hop that real-time applications feel; your entire prompt history transiting and potentially leaking from a third party’s infrastructure. A new single point of failure in an ecosystem where small gateway projects get abandoned routinely.
The template providers should be copying is boring and proven: cloud computing. AWS meters to the CPU-hour but wraps it in calculators, dashboards, and budget alerts, so you forecast before committing and watch it accrue.
Video Game economies set an even higher bar — fixed, known cost for a stated outcome, so scarcity creates desire instead of resentment. Most AI credit systems offer a balance and a reset date. That’s it.
This matters because the next competitive risk isn’t capability — it’s trust.
This matters because the next competitive risk isn’t capability — it’s trust. Models will keep leapfrogging each other; perceived pricing fairness will compound. A provider that routes lightweight tasks to cheap models by default, shows the meter, and forecasts your month earns something rivals can’t ship in a release. One that leans on abstraction to obscure cost is running the casino’s playbook — and there’s a darker extension waiting if the credit economy proves lucrative enough: premium tiers for information access itself, a tax on curiosity that deserves this same scrutiny.
The two things nobody has built
Rollover
Every major provider’s monthly credit allowance expires at reset. Anthropic, Adobe, Google, and Microsoft all document it explicitly: no rollover, no transfer, no pooling. The closest thing to an exception is prepaid top-up packs (OpenAI’s credits last twelve months from purchase), but those still expire and can’t be transferred — a longer leash, not rollover. Given the gym-membership economics above, that’s not an oversight; it’s the business model.
It’s also the one place “future development” language is still honest, and the single change that would most repair the trust problem.
Sub-task routing
Today’s routers pick a model per request. The more interesting frontier splits a single request into parts, sends each to the cheapest capable model, and stitches the results against one goal. Not the autonomous agent currently in vogue, something closer to a compiler: plan up front, execute each piece at minimum cost, assemble the result. The gateways prove the routing works across tasks. Nobody has taken it inside the task.

Author’s note: This analysis & outline reflects my own view, developed with input from Claude and ChatGPT.
